@ssbarnea, I think @martinjuhasz may have misunderstood what you're experiencing. The client *is* supposed to use the original `live` directory, update the symlinks within it, and not make a new one.

While it is possible to manually modify the symlinks and directories, I wouldn't recommend that because it's very easy to get into an inconsistent state. (For reference, you would move the new PEM files from `archive/domain-0001/foo1.pem` into `archive/domain/foo2.pem` for each `foo` in `cert`, `chain`, `fullchain`, and `privkey`, and then change each symlink in `live/foo.pem` to point to the corresponding `../archive/foo2.pem` instead of `../archive/foo1.pem`, and then delete `live/domain-0001` and `archive/domain-0001` and `conf/domain-0001.conf`. However, this is a risky operation because getting any part of the process wrong could confuse the client in a way that's difficult to recover from.)

Did you rerun the client with `-d` options for *each and every* domain name in the old cert, not a subset or a superset of them? That's necessary right now in order to get the client to realize that it should treat the request as a renewal and save the resulting cert as a successor, and many people have found this behavior unintuitive. For example, not specifying a `www.` with a `-d` option (or specifying one if it wasn't specified in the original cert) might not produce the renewal behavior you expect.

I'm working on a `renew` verb for the client that will find certs that need to be renewed and renew them, without having to specify the domains with `-d`. But this hasn't landed in a release yet.

I think problem in regular expression that is used in `CKEDITOR.basePath`: 
`var a = /(^|.*[\\\/])ckeditor\.js(?:\?.*|;.*)?$/i`
In `basePath()` if not defined `window.CKEDITOR_BASEPATH` we iterate over all script tags in head and сheck if they satisfy the regular expression.
Your solution is not the best, because the main reasone to use ManifestFilesMixin is to use HTTP headers Expires and Cache-Control, but in fact if you change ckeditor.js browser will not load new version of file.
A good solution to this problem would be to change this regular expression to match file name.
I used script tag before ckeditor.js to fix problem: 
`<script>window.CKEDITOR_BASEPATH = "{% static 'ckeditor/ckeditor/' %}";</script>` (you need to load static template tag).
Unfortunately you can not write this code to ckeditor-init.js or config.js because they load after ckeditor.js and in ckeditor.js script tries to load resources and gets an error.

I have a full solution to this problem, which involves rewriting the function to remove recursions from the cell graph, and avoids the problem that a reconstructed function's closure can't refer to itself.

I've forked the repo here:
https://github.com/emlynoregan/yccloudpickle

In save_function_tuple(), here
https://github.com/emlynoregan/yccloudpickle/blob/master/yccloudpickle/yccloudpickle.py

I've added a call to the Combinator() function, to modify the function if necessary:
func = Combinator(func)

and I've put the code for Combinator() at the bottom.

The solution is non obvious! More information in this article:
[Automatically Serialising Recursive Inner Functions in Python using the Y Combinator](https://medium.com/@emlynoregan/automatically-serialising-recursive-inner-functions-in-python-using-the-y-combinator-fc5d37e50b29#.of57pdbqb)

I've also added a couple of tests that would throw the "excessively deep recursion" error in cloudpickle, but which yccloudpickle fixes.

I'd love someone to take a look; are you interested in incorporating this into cloudpickle proper?

Anyone who wants to experiment with it:

pip install yccloudpickle
...
import yccloudpickle
yccloudpickle.dumps(f)

etc. I've changed all the names in the package so you can have cloudpickle and yccloudpickle installed side-by-side.

By default RiveScript stores all user variables and state in memory, the bug exists when using Redis cache to store user variables

Here my flask app as an example with Redis as session storage. Short discussions won't work with it as it would store something that is not JSON-serializable.
```
#!/usr/bin/env python

# Manipulate sys.path to be able to import rivescript from this local git
# repository.
import os
import sys
sys.path.append(os.path.join(os.path.dirname(__file__), "..", ".."))

from flask import Flask, request, Response, jsonify
import json
from rivescript import RiveScript
from redis_storage import RedisSessionStorage

# Set up the RiveScript bot. This loads the replies from `/eg/brain` of the
# git repository.
bot = RiveScript(
    session_manager=RedisSessionStorage()
)
bot.load_directory(
    os.path.join(os.path.dirname(__file__), "..", "brain")
)
bot.sort_replies()

app = Flask(__name__)

@app.route("/reply", methods=["POST"])
def reply():
    """Fetch a reply from RiveScript.

    Parameters (JSON):
    * username
    * message
    * vars
    """
    params = request.json
    if not params:
        return jsonify({
            "status": "error",
            "error": "Request must be of the application/json type!",
        })

    username = params.get("username")
    message  = params.get("message")
#   uservars = params.get("vars", dict())

    # Make sure the required params are present.
    if username is None or message is None:
        return jsonify({
            "status": "error",
            "error": "username and message are required keys",
        })

    # Get a reply from the bot.
    reply = bot.reply(username, message)

    # Send the response.
    return reply 

@app.route("/")
@app.route("/<path:path>")
def index(path=None):
    """On all other routes, just return an example `curl` command."""

The third work is the most relevant. I have sent an email to Reynolds who has recently implemented a SAT solver for SL (without general inductive predicates) in CVC4.

The first work (Botincan, Parkinson, Schulte) uses a SMT solver in entailment checking. It proposes an entailment procedure based on Smallfoot. It invokes a SMT solver (e.g., Z3) when it stucks at the frame inference rule: \pure_1 | FRAME |- \pure_2 | EMPTY. This work did not implement a SAT solver for SL in SMT.

The second work (GRASShoper - Piskac Wies Zufferey) reduces a separation logic formula into a first-order logic formula and invokes a SMT solver to discharge the first-order formula. In a correspondent, I remember Wies confirmed that GRASShoper system does not have a stand-alone decision procedure.

I'm surprised to find that there are already methods not only for pretty-printing `pi`, but also for computing increasingly narrow bounds for `pi`.  This can be seen by building the unit tests shipped with the Z3 source, and running `./test-z3 interval` and `./test-z3 rcf` from the build directory.  Relevant source and test files include:
```
    src/math/realclosure/realclosure.cpp
    src/test/rcf.cpp
    src/test/trigo.cpp
    src/test/interval.cpp
```
Computing increasingly narrow bounds like this makes a lot of sense not only for pretty-printing, but also for proving inequalities mixing rational, algebraic, and transcendental numbers.  This raises a few questions:
1) Why doesn't `pp.decimal` work for printing an approximate value of `pi` from the SMT-LIB interface, given that methods are already implemented to do this?  This seems like a bug.

> that diverges from the notebook format

There's two things here:

1. does running the notebook in nteract produce *the exact same notebook* as the existing notebook app
2. does it produce a *valid* notebook

I think it is okay to diverge from the existing notebook implementation on some things like this. For instance, the current notebook *chooses* to merge consecutive stream outputs (from the same stream), but not doing so is perfectly valid, and indeed we made this change in the notebook for performance reasons without changing the notebook format.

In nteract, you could provide merged rendering of stream output by either

1. matching the collation behavior of the existing notebook in the data structure, just merge them for rendering, or
2. actually discarding stderr information, and turn the notebook into purely stdout, matching the primitive behavior of terminals.

My first inclination is toward (1), but if you want to get rigorous terminal-emulation of things like carriage returns (Jupyter's implementation does not behave identically to a terminal), you can't treat them separately at all.

I would agree with @Alexeyan.

> The error is raised while trying to lift a block, which is not really what is intended to do with block.instruction_addrs.

It is neither what one might expect while trying to get a list of instructions if we a talking about the blocks that are stored in the `Function.blocks`. As far as I understand, the `Function.blocks` is meant to represent a set of the "basic blocks" that are part of the function. Those "blocks" are not necessarily the IRSBs; they could be hooked nodes as well. So, it would be somewhat unexpected for them to throw any errors that are related to the lifting process.

I think the proper solution is to return an empty set if the actual block doesn't contain any instructions.

Hi again :)

* Offline-first: do you mean [this](http://kuzzleio.github.io/sdk-documentation/#offline-mode)? :-)
* Decentralized discovery: we don't support data zones yet
* Conflict resolution: we simply expose [ElasticSearch Conflict Management](https://www.elastic.co/guide/en/elasticsearch/guide/current/version-control.html). Usually, it's a "last update wins" strategy, but several options are available to fine-tune this. For instance, updates on critical documents can be done using the '_version' tag, telling ElasticSearch that we want to update a specific version of the document. If the current document version is greater than the provided one, a conflict error occurs.
* Bulk: indeed, we support only bulk insert/update/delete for now. Data dumps functionalities are planned for a later time
* Validation: it seems that you're referring to data *format* validation, through a schema. We call them ``mappings`` and we [support them](https://github.com/kuzzleio/kuzzle/blob/master/docs/API.WebSocket.md#setting-up-a-data-mapping-in-a-collection) (and also in our [SDK](http://kuzzleio.github.io/sdk-documentation/#kuzzledatamapping)). I thought you were talking about functional validation, which is business-specific. For instance, it's very common for a back-end to allow data to be created by an application only if this data complies to certain conditions. That's why I mentioned plugins
* Fine-grained permissions: like I said, this functionality is still in alpha-stage. For instance, a new API route has just been submitted in this [pull request](https://github.com/kuzzleio/kuzzle/pull/83), allowing to create or update roles through the API.
As far as I can tell, it hasn't yet been.

The Makefile is the relevant part for that - when -Wall and -Wextra are on (and -Wno-unknown-pragmas isn't set) it reveals the following warning:
src/image.cpp:513:0: warning: ignoring #pragma omp parallel [-Wunknown-pragmas]
 #pragma omp parallel for schedule(dynamic)

The fix for this is either add -Wno-unknown-pragmas, which leaves it ignored (which is what I did to avoid changing behavior in a pull request whose purpose was to clean up warnings), or add -fopenmp (in both the compile and link stages) which would make it do what was intended. Or remove the #pragma from the file, I suppose, would be a third option. :)

My expectations for this situation would be: 
If your field `timeout` on InputObjectType is nullable & has a default_value defined. If `timeout=None` is passed by the user then I think the user's explicit passing of None should be respected and the default_value not take effect. The value is None. However, if the user omits passing the argument `timeout` completely then I think the default_value should take effect. 

If `timeout` is not nullable & has default_value defined, then as a user I prefer that if I were to explicitly do `timeout=None` it would error because it probably means there's a bug in my code that resulted in None getting passed instead of an integer. I believe this is how it currently works.

I'm not sure that `goog.html.sanitize.HtmlSanitizer` is what we want. We want to transform `"hi"` to `"<strong>hi</strong>"`, but HtmlSanitizer will happily act as the identity on that input. The purpose of `HtmlSanitizer` is to sanitize an actual HTML string, returning an actual HTML string. For instance: * `'test'` is unchanged by `sanitize`, but * `'test'` becomes `'test'`. By contrast, we really just want to embed a plain-text string into HTML: _escape_ or _quote_ would be a better term than _sanitize_. For that purpose, all that you need to do is replace `<` and `&` with their entities. (In the code that you linked, I also replace `>`, but as noted that is just because I didn't like the asymmetry of escaping `<` but not `>`! It's not necessary.) [HTML text nodes have no other metacharacters](https://html.spec.whatwg.org/multipage/parsing.html#data-state), so this is sufficient. We can definitely still have a standard `function escapeHtmlText(x: string): string`—name up for grabs, but it should be clear that the result should be used only in a text node context as opposed to, say, an attribute context. ([Properly supporting attribute escaping is tricky.](http://wonko.com/post/html-escaping)) This function should go into [`tf_utils/utils.ts`](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorboard/blob/2615072a3e46a321b163410dea5eeaf43e9272be/tensorboard/components/tf_utils/utils.ts). Alternatively, if you can find a suitable closure-library function that does the right thing, I'd be very happy with that, too!

> it really only guarantees that A won't be admitted for garbage collection before B, but doesn't guarantee that they can't be admitted together

Not exactly. B isn't identified as garbage in the garbage collection pass, because its reference is held by pybind11. It's really about the order of clearing A vs calling the weakref callbacks on A's weakref, which isn't guaranteed. If A is cleared first, everything goes fine. If the weakref callback is called first, then it drops the last reference to B, immediately causing B to be cleared.

> I'm not sure that there's a simple workaround that could be done to deal with this on the pybind11 side (especially since neither object actually has to be pybind-managed).

I hadn't considered that the objects aren't necessarily pybind-managed, but on the other hand if the nurse isn't a C++ object then I don't think this is an issue. I have some ideas for fixing it if it is pybind-managed, which I can experiment with. Is there a way to determine whether a given py::handle is pybind-managed?

@wjakob Would you consider reverting the `forcecast` default (or changing it to `c_style`)? This is an "implicit versus explicit" kind of issue -- if your function accepts arrays of ints, it probably wants just that. If it expects a structured type and you give it a bunch of ints, it's likely a user error and you don't want those values to be copied over all fields. Also, (as with the example in this issue) the overloads may not work as you would expect them to unless you disable the forcecast; I believe it's quite counter-intuitive from C++ point of view.

It gets even more weird once you start working with structured arrays. NumPy will happily cast anything to anything according to a set of strange conventions. Chances are, you'll *never* want to see any of this happen accidentally. Just a few examples:

```python
# cast ints to structs
>>> a = np.array([1, 2, 3], dtype='u2')
>>> a.astype([('a', 'u4'), ('b', 'u1')], casting='unsafe')
array([(1, 1), (2, 2), (3, 3)], 
      dtype=[('a', '<u4'), ('b', 'u1')])

# happily cast integer structs to floats, discarding half of the data
>>> np.rec.fromarrays([[1, 2, 3], [4, 5, 6]]).astype('f4', casting='unsafe')
rec.array([ 1.,  2.,  3.], 
          dtype=float32)

# strings to bools, no problem
>>> np.array(['-1', '0', '1']).astype('bool', casting='unsafe')
array([ True, False,  True], dtype=bool)
```

I had deadlocks when C++ threads were calling Python code when they attempted to aquire the GIL using `gil_scoped_acquire`. As @jagerman mentions, my problem was that the thread was launched by a C++ function called from Python, which still had the GIL. I added the following guard policy to the call: `pb::call_guard<pb::gil_scoped_release>()`, so that the calling thread would release the GIL, and the deadlock was fixed.

I think the point of bundling should be bundling of input and output files, not necessarily the intermediate files, and the primary purpose should be for reproducible analysis. That is to say, if we bundle a project, the project should be able to reproduce by itself, except for certain verifiable requirements (executables, remote hosts). That is to say, if we can include the following in bundles

1. meta data (keywords, descriptions etc)
2. "external" requirements (executable targets, reference genome files, etc)
3. input and output data

The bundled package should be reproducible as long as the external requirements are met.

If we leave "external" requirements outside of bundle, we can create tar files with required files, and allow the reproducibility of bundles when external requirements are met. If we would like to keep "external" requirements, we should create docker images for the bundles so that the workflow can be executed. I am not sure how to achieve the latter though.

There is a minor difference here: we claim the "connectedness" of workflow steps so in `sos_run('a')` we know that `a_2` would inherit output from `a_1`. In `sos_step('a_1')`, `sos_step)('a_2')` (or `sos_step('a')` as you proposed), the steps are **not connected** and are executed as separate steps.

**More recent instructions.**

**Note:** Throughout, you will need to replace `<head-node-ip>` with something more correct.

The processes on the **head node** can be started as follows.

1. Run the following in Python to start the Redis shards.

    ```python
    import ray

    ray.services.start_redis("<head-node-ip>",
                             port=6379,
                             num_redis_shards=1,
                             redirect_output=False,
                             cleanup=False)

2. Start a global scheduler.

    ```
    ./python/ray/core/src/global_scheduler/global_scheduler \
        -r <head-node-ip>:6379 \
        -h <head-node-ip>
    ```

3. Start a plasma store

    ```
    ./python/ray/core/src/plasma/plasma_store \
        -s /tmp/s1 \
        -m 1000000000
    ```

4. Start a plasma manager

    ```
    ./python/ray/core/src/plasma/plasma_manager \
        -s /tmp/s1 \
        -m /tmp/m1 \
        -r <head-node-ip>:6379 \
        -h <head-node-ip> \
        -p 23894
    ```

5. Start the local scheduler

    ```
    # Without ability to start new workers.
    ./python/ray/core/src/local_scheduler/local_scheduler \
        -s /tmp/sched1 \
        -p /tmp/s1 \
        -m /tmp/m1 \
        -r <head-node-ip>:6379 \
        -a <head-node-ip>:23894 \
        -h <head-node-ip>

    # With ability to start new workers.
    ./python/ray/core/src/local_scheduler/local_scheduler \
        -s /tmp/sched1 \
        -p /tmp/s1 \
        -m /tmp/m1 \
        -r <head-node-ip>:6379 \
        -a <head-node-ip>:23894 \
        -h <head-node-ip> \
        -w "python python/ray/workers/default_worker.py --redis-address <head-node-ip>:6379 --object-store-name /tmp/s1 --object-store-manager-name /tmp/m1 --local-scheduler-name /tmp/sched1 --node-ip-address <head-node-ip>"
    ```

6. Start a worker (or run this multiple times to start multiple workers).

   ```
    python python/ray/workers/default_worker.py \
        --redis-address <head-node-ip>:6379 \
        --object-store-name /tmp/s1 \
        --object-store-manager-name /tmp/m1 \
        --local-scheduler-name /tmp/sched1 \
        --node-ip-address <head-node-ip>
    ```

7. Connect a driver (run this in a Python interpreter).

    ```python
    import ray
    ray.init(redis_address="<head-node-ip>:6379")
    ```

**After that, it should be possible to start up Ray on other nodes as follows.***

```
ray start --redis-address=<head-node-ip>:6379
```

@ejeschke -- I just pushed a few changes:

+ Added an option `prevent_default_action` for `DOMListener`.
+ Added several more events, including `contextmenu`.

AFAICT in chrome, a right-click generates the event `contextmenu`, so that is now a watchable event. If the `DOMListener` instance has `prevent_default_action=True` then the default action (e.g. context menu, or dragging) will not occur.

The snippet below shows how to prevent the default and disable dragging. I put the `dragstart` watcher into a separate listener because I don't really care about its events....I just want to prevent the default action. It could be included in the `im_events` listener instead.

```python
image = Image(value=value, format='png')

im_events = DOMListener()
im_events.source = image
im_events.watched_events = ['click', 'contextmenu']
im_events.prevent_default_action = True

no_drag = DOMListener(source=image, watched_events=['dragstart'], prevent_default_action = True)
```

Hi Gioele,

Thanks for following up, and I apologize if I didn't make myself clear above. In my understanding, rather than modifying the NH tag, cellranger only modifies the MAPQ score. When it makes the adjustment, which is done at the read level for multi-mapped reads if one is exonic, it switches the MAPQ score of the exonic alignment to MAPQ 255 and adds the MM:i:1 tag and changes the MAPQ scores for the other non-exonic alignments to 0.

Since it doesn't modify the NH tag, if you still filter by excluding reads with NH > 1, then you should exclude all these reads:
```
samtools view -q 255 -@ $SLURM_NPROCS neurons_900_possorted_genome_bam.bam | grep "MM:" | wc -l
5288191

samtools view -q 255 -@ $SLURM_NPROCS neurons_900_possorted_genome_bam.bam | grep "RE:A:E" | wc -l
34373614

5288191/34373614 * 100
15.38444866460652

samtools view -q 255 neurons_900_possorted_genome_bam.bam | grep -v "MM" | cut -f12 | sort | uniq -c
41794226 NH:i:1

samtools view -q 255 neurons_900_possorted_genome_bam.bam |  grep "MM" | cut -f12 | sort | uniq -c
3478977 NH:i:2
 977004 NH:i:3
 402723 NH:i:4
 158749 NH:i:5
 132911 NH:i:6
  85977 NH:i:7
  35022 NH:i:8
   9650 NH:i:9
   7178 NH:i:10

samtools view neurons_900_possorted_genome_bam.bam | grep "NH:i:1\b" | cut -f 5 | sort | uniq -c
41794226 255
```
Therefore all the reads that have the NH:i:1 tag are uniquely mapped, but not all the reads with a MAPQ of 255 are (even though in most bam files they should be). Since `velocyto.py` uses the NH tag, it looks like by default you're actually excluding all these "MAPQ-adjusted-unique reads", which I agree makes the most sense. 

It may be true that some of these multimapped reads actually originated from introns. This is supported by the fact that I've often seen separate reads with the same cell barcode / UMI be uniquely mapped (without the MAPQ adjustment) to intronic locations, but this should still be captured after the NH tag filtering of the ambiguous multimapped reads.

The last thing I'll note is that the 10x `bcfile` used by `velocyto.py` contains a list of barcodes that are identified based on UMI counts that include these inflated number of uniquely-aligned transcripts, but I haven't thought or tested too carefully whether this procedure causes any biases in determining which cell barcodes to include. For example, cells whose reads have a higher percentage of multimapped reads may appear to have more exonic UMIs (coming either from double-counting of the same UMI to multiple genes or counting intronic UMIs as exonics) than they actually do.

Best,
David
